frameworks.name: sensory recruitment hypothesis
frameworks.one_line: early visual cortex supports both perception and working memory maintenance

frameworks.name: retinotopic feature representation
frameworks.one_line: memory representations preserve spatial specificity when binding is required

frameworks.name: nonretinotopic working memory
frameworks.one_line: claim that V1 holds orientation information independent of location

core_question: Do early visual areas maintain working memory representations in a spatially specific, retinotopic manner?
key_assumptions: fMRI multivariate patterns index feature-specific memory traces
early visual cortex serves as substrate for both perception and short-term retention
task demands modulate whether features are bound to spatial locations
manipulations: two laterally presented gratings held in memory simultaneously
comparison across V1, V2, and higher extrastriate areas
key_findings: orientation of each remembered grating classified more accurately from contralateral V1 and V2
higher extrastriate areas showed similar classification across hemispheres
retinotopic specificity in V1/V2 persisted throughout the retention interval
interpretations: early visual cortex can maintain feature information in a spatially specific manner
task-induced feature-location binding shapes retinotopic organization of working memory
